segments.s055-g81: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. از پیش تختش is a prepositional phrase 'from before his throne' with تختش = تخت + possessive -ش 'his'. گرازید here is a past-form verb meaning 'withdrew/left' (poetic/archaic usage); Sam is the named subject placed after the verb (typical flexible word order in epic verse).
segments.s055-g82: پسش is an adverbial/locative 'behind him/after him' (the -ش refers back to Sam). پهلوانان is the plural subject 'the champions/heroes'. نهادند is the past plural verb 'they placed/set' (نهادن used here as an action verb). گام is the noun 'step' and forms the object/compliment: گام نهادن = 'to set a step', i.e. to advance/follow.
segments.s055-g83: خرامید is a past singular verb 'he strode/strutted' (implied subject). و is the coordinating conjunction 'and'. شد is the past of شدن used here to mean 'turned/moved (became toward)', followed by سوی + noun to mark direction: سوی آرامگاه = 'toward the tomb/resting-place'. Word order is poetic but straightforward: verb(s) then direction phrase.
segments.s055-g84: همی is the classical continuous/iterative particle (here adding an ongoing or habitual aspect: 'was/kept on'). کرد is the main past verb 'did/acted'. گیتی is the subject 'the world'. به + آیین و راه is a prepositional phrase meaning 'in/according to custom and way' (به here marking the manner or means). The clause thus says the world acted in accordance with established custom and way.
segments.s055-g65: Tu (تو) is the subject 'you'. از باستان is an ablative phrase 'from antiquity' modifying یادگار 'a relic/memento'. منی is composed of من (my/me) plus the final poetic copula -ی meaning 'are' — together: 'you are my memento.'
segments.s055-g66: به تخت کئی بر is a locative phrase: به + تخت 'to / at the throne' with کئی (Kayanian) qualifying تخت and بر functioning as 'upon/on'. بهار منی is the predicate 'you are my spring', again with the possessive notion (من) plus the copula -ی. Overall: 'on the Kai-throne you are my spring.'
segments.s055-g67: به رزم اندرون is a locative expression 'in the field of battle / in war'. شیر is the predicate noun 'lion' and پاینده‌ای is an adjectival predicate with the final -ی serving as the copula in 2nd person: 'you are everlasting/long‑lived.' The subject 'you' is omitted and understood from the copula.
segments.s055-g68: به بزم اندرون = 'in the banquet/assembly'. شید is a predicate meaning 'beloved' or 'love‑inspired' and تابنده‌ای is an adjectival predicate with the copula -ی: 'you are shining/radiant.' Again the subject 'you' is implied by the verb ending.
segments.s055-g69: زمین و زمان is a paired subject 'earth and time'. خاک پای تو ('the dust at your feet') is the predicate, and باد is the optative particle 'may it be' (classical/poetic wish). Word order places the wished-for state after the subjects for emphasis.
segments.s055-g70: همان تخت پیروزه is a nominal phrase 'that very/that same turquoise (or jeweled) throne'; جای تو is 'your place/seat'. باد again is the optative 'may it be', yielding 'may that throne be your place.'
segments.s055-g71: تو شستی uses شستی (past of شستن) in a figurative/transitive sense 'you have cleansed' or 'you washed/cleansed' with the object زمین 'the land'. به شمشیر هندی is an instrumental phrase 'with the Indian sword' (به marking the instrument). Word order is poetic, placing instrument before the object.
segments.s055-g72: به آرام بنشین is an imperative: به آرام 'calmly / in peace' + بنشین 'sit down / settle'. و links the clauses, and رامش گزین is a second imperative 'choose tranquility' (گزین = choose/select).
segments.s055-g41: This nominal phrase uses the masdar-like infinitive برافراختن ('to raise') + direct object سر ('head') with به بیشی acting adverbially to mean 'in excess / proudly'. The conjunction و links a second coordinated noun گنج ('treasure'), so the whole segment lists two proud/avaricious acts: 'raising his head (in pride) and (amassing) treasure.'
segments.s055-g42: This is the continuation of the predicative nominal phrase: به + رنجور مردم ('to/for the ailing people') marks the beneficiary or target, followed by نماینده رنج lit. 'representative of suffering' used as a noun phrase meaning 'a source/embodiment of suffering'. Word order places the target before the predicate noun.
segments.s055-g43: همه ('all/everyone') is fronted; نزد من ('in my presence / to me') gives the speaker's perspective. The idiomatic phrase سر به سر here intensifies the predicate (literally 'head to head') and is followed by the verbless predication turned into کافرند ('they are unbelievers'), so the clause means 'all, as I see it, are utterly unbelievers.'
segments.s055-g44: و introduces a coordinating clause; ز here = 'because of / from' and links the cause (آهرمن, Ahriman) to the judgment. بدکنش ('ill‑doer') is a noun describing those people and بدترند is the verbal predicate 'they are worse.' The whole clause says they are even worse as evildoers, on account of Ahriman.
segments.s055-g45: Relative clause introduced by هر آن کس که ('whoever/each one who'); او here is the resumptive/heavy pronoun referring to that person. جز بر این دین بود literally 'was except on this religion' = 'was not of this religion.' بود is the past copula marking the state.
segments.s055-g46: This clause gives the consequence: ز یزدان ('from/by God') and از منش ('and from/by his character/origin') mark sources/reasons for نفرین بود ('was a curse / was accursed'). The copula بود again places the state in the past; the sequence means 'was accursed by God and (by) his nature.'
segments.s055-g47: و زان پس ('and thereafter / from that then') gives temporal sequence. به شمشیر ('by/with the sword') marks instrument; یازیم is a first‑person-future or present-future verb form here ('I will break forth / send forth'), and دست ('hand') is the direct object or idiomatic object meaning 'reach out / take action.' Together: 'thereafter I will, with the sword, put forth my hand.'
segments.s055-g48: کنم is the first‑person singular present/future of کردن ('I will do / I make'); the coordinated phrase سر به سر کشور و مرز پست uses سر به سر idiomatically as 'one and all / completely' and names the objects کشور ('country') and مرز پست ('low/basest border'). The clause means 'I will (thus) subdue/conquer the entire realm and its low border.'
segments.s055-g49: همه پهلوانان is a quantified noun phrase: همه (quantifier) precedes پهلوانان (plural noun) meaning “all the heroes.” روی زمین is a locative prepositional phrase (روی = “on/upon” + زمین = “earth/ground”) placed after the noun phrase to mean “in the world/on earth.”
segments.s055-g50: منوچهر را is the definite object marked by را (object marker) placed before the verb, so Manuchehr is the one receiving the action. خواندند is past tense, 3rd-person plural (they read/sang/cried), here used to mean they cried out or chanted; آفرین is the interjection/content of what they cried (an acclamation).
segments.s055-g51: که here introduces an explanatory/relative clause (“that”/“for”/“who”); فرخ is an epithet meaning “fortunate” modifying نیای (نیای تو = “your ancestor”). تو‌ای نیک‌خواه is a vocative address — تو‌ای is poetic 'O you' and نیک‌خواه (‘well-wisher’/‘benevolent’) is the addressee’s epithet.
segments.s055-g52: تو را داد is an SOV pattern with the object (تو + object marker را) before the past-tense verb داد (“gave”). شاهی و تخت و کلاه is a noun list of what was given: شاهی (kingship), تخت (throne), and کلاه (hat/crown); و simply coordinates the items.
segments.s055-g53: تو را باد is an optative construction where باد functions as a jussive/optative particle meaning “may (it be) for you.” جاوید modifies تخت to yield “eternal throne.” ردان is an adjectival/participial modifier attached to تخت (poetic word-order), here meaning something like “repelling/expelling” and so qualifies the throne.
segments.s055-g54: همان is an emphatic/demonstrative “that same” linking to what was just said. تاج و هم فره موبدان lists two items: تاج (“crown”) and فره موبدان, where فره (“farr,” divine glory) is in a genitive / construct relation with موبدان (mōbeds, Zoroastrian priests); the ezāfe is omitted as common in epic poetry.
segments.s055-g55: دل ما یکایک is a subject phrase: دل ما (“our heart/heart of us”) followed by یکایک (“each and every”), so it means “each of our hearts.” به فرمان تو است is the predicate: به + فرمان تو = “to/under your command” and است is the present copula (“is”), giving “is at your command.”
segments.s055-g56: همان at the start echoes/links to the previous clause (“likewise/the same”). جان ما is “our life(s)/very lives,” and زیر پیمان تو است literally means “is under your pledge/covenant” (زیر = under/subject to, پیمان = pledge/covenant, تو = your, است = is).
segments.s055-g17: This is a nominal phrase followed by the copula 'منم' (I am). 'شب تار' = 'dark night' (noun + adjective; Persian often puts the adjective after the noun). 'جوینده کین' is a noun + noun (construct/apposition) where 'جوینده' is a present-participle used attributively ('seeker') and 'کین' ('vengeance') is the thing sought; in poetry the ezafe linking them is often omitted. 'منم' is the emphatic present copula 'I am' (م attached to the verb).
segments.s055-g18: 'همان' is a demonstrative/emphatic ('that' / 'the very') placed before the noun. 'آتش تیز برزین' is a noun phrase: 'آتش' (fire) modified by the adjective 'تیز' (keen/sharp) and further specified by the proper name 'برزین' (Barzin) in apposition/possessive relation (poetic omission of ezafe). The line ends with 'منم' again as the copula 'I am', identifying the speaker with this image.
segments.s055-g19: 'خداوند' here functions as an honorific/epithet meaning 'lord' or 'master' rather than the monotheistic 'God'. It governs the following nouns as possessions or attributes: 'شمشیر' (sword) and 'زرینه کفش' (golden shoe), with 'و' linking the two objects. The phrase is nominal and sets up a title; there is no copula in this segment.
segments.s055-g20: 'فرازنده' is a present-participle used attributively ('one who raises' / 'exalter'). It modifies the nominal 'کاویانی درفش' = 'Kayanian banner/standard' (again the ezafe is omitted in poetic diction). Together they form a predicate-apposition to the previous nominal title: 'lord of ... , raiser of the Kayanian banner.'
segments.s055-g21: Two coordinated nominal phrases describing weapons: 'فروزنده میغ' ('the bright/illuminating blade') and 'برنده تیغ' ('the cutting/keen sword'). 'فروزنده' and 'برنده' are participial/adjectival forms modifying the following nouns; the conjunction 'و' links the two descriptive phrases.
segments.s055-g22: This clause states a negative possession: 'ندارم' = 'I do not have.' The prepositional phrase 'به جنگ' ('into/for war') together with 'اندرون جان' ('within the inner self' / 'in my soul') gives the domain where the lack is felt; 'دریغ' ('hesitation, reluctance') is the thing not possessed. Word order is slightly inverted for poetic effect: literally 'to war, inside the soul, I have not hesitation.'
segments.s055-g23: 'گه' is an adverb meaning 'sometimes.' 'بزم دریا' is a poetic genitive/compound ('the sea's feast/revelry' or 'assembly of the sea') with the ezafe omitted; 'دو دست من است' is an equational clause 'are my two hands' — together the phrase says, idiomatically, that at times the tumult or play of the sea is within his two hands (a poetic way to boast of strength or control).
segments.s055-g24: 'دم' here means 'breath' or 'blast.' The compound 'دم آتش' = 'breath/blast of fire.' 'از بر نشست' is a poetic locative phrasing meaning 'sat down beside' or 'took place at the side of' (the preposition 'از' + 'بر' gives a sense of position; 'نشست' is the past/perfective verbal noun 'sat/settled'). The clause ends with 'من است' ('is mine' / 'is by me'), so overall 'the breath of fire has sat at my side.'
segments.s055-g1: Subject + duration + state: منوچهر is the subject, یک هفته ('for a week') is a temporal/duration phrase placed after the subject, با درد is a prepositional phrase meaning 'with pain' (idiomatically 'in pain' or 'afflicted'), and بود is the past-tense copula 'was' at the end of the clause. The overall order is typical Persian (S ... adjunct ... V).
segments.s055-g2: Possessive noun + predicate: دو چشمش = 'his two eyes' (دو before the noun, -ش is the possessive suffix 'his'). پر آب is a predicative phrase 'full of water' (پر 'full' + noun آب), and then و coordinates the following predication رخش زرد بود, where رخش ('his countenance' here) + adjective زرد ('pale') and the copula بود ('was') appear at the end; the final verb governs both coordinated predications in this poetic line.
segments.s055-g3: Temporal prepositional phrase + verb + title: به هشتم functions as a temporal phrase 'on the eighth (day)' placed before the verb; بیامد is the past tense 'came'; منوچهر شاه (name + title) follows, a common poetic word order where the subject comes after the verb.
segments.s055-g4: Locative phrase + verb + object in poetic order: به سر = 'upon (the) head', بر نهاد is a compound where بر (on/upon) + نهاد (past of 'to place') = 'placed on', and آن کیانی کلاه is the object 'that Kayanian hat' in a slightly inverted/poetic order (demonstrative + adjective + noun). The verb نهاد is the past action placing the hat on the head.
segments.s055-g5: Quantifier + noun + locative: همه پهلوانان = 'all the champions' (همه before the noun), and روی زمین is a locative phrase 'on the earth' (روی functioning as 'on/upon' with زمین 'earth/ground'). This is a topical/locative specification of who and where.
segments.s055-g6: Target phrase + adverb + past plural verb + interjection: بر او = 'to him' (بر indicating direction/target plus pronoun او), یکسره is an adverb 'altogether/completely' modifying the action, خواندند is the past plural 'they uttered/declared', and آفرین is the exclamation 'Bravo' or 'acclamation' that they voiced. The plural verb implies an omitted plural subject ('they/all the champions').
segments.s055-g7: Temporal conjunction + nominal compound + locative verb: چو is the conjunction 'when', دیهیم شاهی is a compound in which دیهیم (rustic/villager) qualifies شاهی ('kingship') — 'the rural/villagers' kingship' — and به سر بر نهاد is the locative verbal phrase 'placed upon (his) head' (به سر 'on the head' + بر نهاد 'placed on'). The verb نهاد is past tense 'placed/assumed'. Note the poetic compactness of the noun phrase.
segments.s055-g8: Direct object marked + adverbial + verb: جهان را uses the object marker را to mark 'the world' as the scope/target, سراسر همه = 'throughout all' (saraasar 'throughout' and همه 'all' reinforcing comprehensiveness), مژده داد is 'gave/announced good news' with داد as the past tense verb 'gave'. The verb at the end is standard Persian word order for a transitive clause.
segments.s055-g25: بدان is an imperative formed with the prefix ب- + دان (know), here functioning as 'know that' (an introductory imperative). را is the object/emphasis marker attached to that clause. ز introduces an ablative/reasonal phrase 'from/because of'; بد دست is a noun phrase 'evil hand' (دست = 'hand' / power), and کوته کنم is a present subjunctive/optative or volitive construction 'I will shorten / I will cut short' with کنَم as the 1st person singular auxiliary 'I do/make'. The whole segment reads as a proclamation: 'Know that I will cut short the evil hand.'
segments.s055-g26: زمین را is the direct object 'the earth' (را marking definiteness). به introduces the manner/colour phrase 'in / with the color of ...' (here 'in' the hue of). کین رنگ is 'the color/hue of vengeance' (کین = vengeance, رنگ = color), دیبه کنم uses دیبه 'silken robe' metaphorically and کنَم ('I make') to mean 'I will clothe/cover (the earth)'. Grammatically this is a causative/result phrase: 'I will clothe the earth in the color of vengeance.'
segments.s055-g27: گراینده is an agentive present-participial form 'one who directs/wields'. It combines with گرز ('mace') as its object: 'the mace-wielder' or literally 'one who directs the mace.' و is the coordinating conjunction 'and'. نماینده تاج is a noun phrase 'representative/holder of the crown' (نماینده = representative, تاج = crown). The order places the agentive/epithet before the thing it governs ('گراینده گرز' = 'mace-wielder').
segments.s055-g28: فروزنده is another agentive/participial adjective 'illuminating' / 'making to shine'. ملک here means 'realm/kingdom' (the object being brightened). بر تخت عاج is a locative phrase 'upon the ivory throne' (بر = on/upon, تخت = throne, عاج = ivory). Together: 'making the kingdom shine upon the ivory throne' — a descriptive clause attached to the epithets in the previous segment.
segments.s055-g29: ابا here is the prepositional 'با' ('with') in an archaic/metrical form introducing the instrument/condition 'with these arts'. این هنرها = 'these arts/skills'. یکی بنده‌ام is a humble predicate: یکی = 'one' (i.e. merely one), بنده‌ام = 'I am a servant' with the first‑person suffix -ام. The whole segment expresses humble self‑characterization: 'With these arts, I am but one servant.'
segments.s055-g30: جهان‌آفرین را = 'the World‑Creator' marked by را as definite object; پرستنده‌ام is the first‑person predicative form meaning 'I am a worshipper / I worship' (پرستنده used as predicate + -ام). Grammatically this is a simple subject‑predicate construction: 'I am a worshipper of the World‑Creator.'
segments.s055-g31: همه is the universal subject 'all (of us)'. دست بر روی گریان is a prepositional/accusative phrase 'hands upon (our) weeping face(s)' (دست = hand, بر = on/upon, روی = face, گریان = weeping). زنیم is the 1st‑person plural present form of زدن used here as 'we strike/beat' — a customary gesture of lamentation (we beat our hands upon our faces). The verb agrees with the plural subject 'همه.'
segments.s055-g32: همه داستان‌ها is 'all the tales/stories' (همه = all, داستان‌ها = tales). ز یزدان is a prepositional phrase 'from God' (ز = from, یزدان = God). زنیم here is the 1st‑person plural of زدن used in the sense 'to recite / to sound / to perform' (i.e. 'we recite/relate'). Thus: 'We recite all tales that are from God' or more simply 'we recite all the tales of God.'
segments.s055-g73: از این پس is an idiom meaning “from now on” (از = from; این = this; پس = after). همه نوبت ماست is a copular clause where نوبت (‘turn’) is the head and ماست (‘is ours’) is the predicate; word order is poetic/inverted so رزم (‘battle’) appears at the end as the domain/qualification of that turn: “the whole turn for battle is ours.”
segments.s055-g74: تو را جای تخت است is a dative-like construction where تو را (topic/emphasis marking of ‘you’) introduces the person for whom something exists; جای تخت است literally ‘a place (on) the throne is’ = ‘you have a place on the throne.’ The conjunctions و link شادی و بزم (‘joy and feast’) as additional things that belong to you. Note the topicalizing را after تو rather than a preposition.
segments.s055-g75: شوم is an optative/subjunctive first‑person form meaning ‘may I become / let me be’; گرد گیتی برآیم is a verbal phrase ‘go about the world’ with گرد (‘around’) + گیتی (‘world’) and برآیم (subjunctive of برآمد/برآورد ‘go up, come forth, set out’). یکی at the end is indefinite ‘one/some person’ (here ‘one [enemy]’).
segments.s055-g76: ز دشمن به بند آورم is a compound verb phrase: ز (‘from’) دشمن (‘enemy’) + به بند آوردن (‘to bring into bond/to capture’). آورم is 1st‑person present/subjunctive ‘I may bring’; اندکی (‘a little’) qualifies the result or duration. The sequence expresses a wish to capture one enemy for a short while.
segments.s055-g77: This is a poetic inversion: مرا پهلوانی نیای تو داد = ‘Your ancestor gave me heroism.’ مرا (me) marks the affected person, پهلوانی (heroism/valour) is the thing given, نیای تو (‘your ancestor’) is the giver, and داد is the past tense ‘gave.’ Note the old/poetic word order where object and theme precede the subject.
segments.s055-g78: دلم را خرد مهر و رای تو داد is another inverted giving construction: دلم را (‘my heart’ with possessive -م and object marker را) is the recipient, خرد (‘wisdom’) is what is given, and مهر و رای تو (‘your love and your counsel’) is the agent/topic; داد is ‘gave.’ Literally: ‘To my heart, wisdom your love and counsel gave.’
segments.s055-g79: بر او آفرین کرد is a standard phrase ‘he made praise upon him’ = ‘he praised him’; بر is the preposition ‘upon,’ او is the object ‘him,’ آفرین here means ‘praise/acclamation,’ and کرد is the past tense ‘did/made.’ بس before شهریار is a poetic intensifier/adverb attached to the subject (here ‘the king’), so the clause reads ‘the king praised him much.’
segments.s055-g80: بسی دادش از گوهر شاهوار: بسی (‘much/abundantly’) modifies دادش (‘gave him’ — داد + enclitic ـش ‘to him’). از گوهر شاهوار = ‘from/of regal jewels’ describes what was given. Together: ‘(He) gave him abundant jewels of royal quality.’
segments.s055-g9: This is a nominal list introduced by the preposition به (here meaning 'in' or 'for'); the first two items repeat به for emphasis ('به داد و به آیین') while the third item (مردانگی) is coordinated by و and understood under the same preposition. داد is the noun 'justice' (not a past-tense verb) and آیین and مردانگی are abstract virtues; there is no verb inside this segment because it is a continued listing of qualities.
segments.s055-g10: Another coordinated noun phrase introduced by به; نیکی, پاکی, فرزانگی are abstract nouns ('goodness', 'purity', 'wisdom') joined by و. The preposition governs the whole series, and فرزانگی is an abstract noun formed with the suffix -گی; again there is no finite verb here, this continues the catalogue of virtues.
segments.s055-g11: منم is an emphatic self-identification 'I am' inside reported speech; گفت here introduces the utterance (classical/poetic presentation of speech). بر تخت گردان سپهر is a prepositional phrase 'on the revolving throne of the sky/heavens' where گردان is a participial/adjectival element 'revolving' modifying تخت; word order and the poetic participial form are characteristic of Ferdowsi's style.
segments.s055-g12: همم is a nominal subject meaning 'my aim/endeavor' or 'my disposition.' It takes the copula است: همم خشم و جنگ است 'my purpose is anger and war.' The phrase ו هم داد و مهر attaches as an additional predication ('and also justice and love'), with هم before داد adding the sense 'also'; است governs both coordinated nouns, creating the contrastive pairing (wrath/war vs. justice/affection).
segments.s055-g13: This is a two-part nominal statement with the final copula من است applying to both predicates: زمین بنده و چرخ یار من است = 'the earth (is) [my] servant and the sky (is) [my] companion.' بنده and یار are predicative nouns linked to زمین and چرخ; من is the possessor ('my'), and است is the copula at the end.
segments.s055-g14: سر تاجداران is a genitive/construction meaning 'the heads of the crown-bearers' (i.e. rulers); شکار من است is 'are my prey/quarry.' سر is used collectively here (singular noun with a general meaning), تاجداران is plural 'those who wear crowns,' and شکار is the predicative noun 'prey.'
segments.s055-g15: همم again is the subject 'my purpose/aspiration.' The clause pairs دین ('religion/faith') with فره ایزدی ('divine glory'); ایزدیست is a poetic fusion of ایزدی + است meaning 'is godly' or 'is of the divine.' Notice the ellipsis: the copula is effectively shared, so the structure reads as 'my purpose is religion, and my farr is divine.'
segments.s055-g16: Here همم again governs two coordinated predicates: بخت نیکی 'good fortune' (بخت = fate/lot/fortune, نیکی = goodness) and بخردیست, a poetic contraction meaning 'is of wisdom' or 'is wise' (بخردی است). The initial هم and the second هم add symmetry and 'also' — 'my lot is good, and I am also wise.'
segments.s055-g57: This is a simple past clause: a titled noun + name (جهان‌پهلوان سام) is the subject, followed by the prepositional phrase بر پای (literally "on [his] foot[s]") and the past verb خاست "arose/stood up." Classical word order places the location/stance (بر پای) before the verb خاست.
segments.s055-g58: چنین (thus) precedes the verb گفت (said) to mark the manner; the following clause begins with the vocative کای ("O") addressing خسرو. داد راست is a nominal phrase in which داد is the noun "justice" and راست qualifies it as "true/right." The whole segment reports speech: "he said thus: 'O Khosrow, justice is true.'"
segments.s055-g59: ز شاهان is a poetic partitive/ablative meaning "of/from the kings." مرا here functions as an experiencer/dative construction (literally "to me"), and دیده ("eye") is the subject with the prepositional complement بر دیدن ("upon seeing/to seeing"). The copula است closes the clause: literally, "Of kings, to me the eye is upon seeing," i.e. "among kings my eye longs to behold."
segments.s055-g60: This clause uses the same poetic source‑first pattern: ز تو داد ("from you is justice") places the source before the copula. The conjunction و links the next clause, and از ما پسندیدن است literally reads "from us is liking/approval is," i.e. "and we approve." پسندیدن is used as a noun/nominal verbal idea here and است completes the predication.
segments.s055-g61: پدر بر پدر is a doubled construction with بر used for intensification: "father upon father" = "father of fathers" (an honorific superlative). شاه ایران is a genitival phrase "king of Iran," and the copula تویی (archaic poetic "thou art") ends the clause, so the order is emphatic (title/epithet before the verb).
segments.s055-g62: گزین here is a qualifying word ("select/choicest") modifying سواران و شیران ("horsemen and lions"—lions as a poetic metaphor for brave men). The clause ends again with تویی, so the whole means "You are the choicest among riders and warriors."
segments.s055-g63: This is an optative sentence: تو را marks the person concerned (accusative/dative), پاک یزدان is the agent/subject "the pure God," نگه‌دار is a verbal root "keep/preserve," and باد is the optative particle meaning "may it be." Together they form the wish "May the pure God keep you."
segments.s055-g64: دلت is دل + the 2nd‑person suffix ت ("your heart"). شادمان is a predicative adjective "joyful/happy." The second small clause بخت بیدار باد repeats the optative explicitly: بخت "fortune/fate," بیدار "awake/alert," and باد again as "may it be." The construction expresses two linked wishes; the first wish omits an explicit باد but is understood from the second.
segments.s055-g33: This is two linked clauses with an implicit parallel structure. 'کز او تاج و تخت است' literally 'from him is crown and throne' (= 'crown and throne come from him'), where 'کز' is the poetic/archaic preposition meaning 'from' and 'است' is the copula putting the predicate after the source. The second short clause 'ز اویم سپاه' omits an explicit verb (the copula) and uses inverted word order: 'ز اویم' ('we are/from him' or 'from him are we') followed by 'سپاه' ('army'), so the sense is 'we are his army' or 'our army is from him.'
segments.s055-g34: 'از اویم سپاس' repeats the same pattern: 'از اویم' marks source/possession ('from him') and 'سپاس' is the nominal predicate ('praise'/'thanks'), with the copula understood rather than stated. 'و بدویم پناه' = 'and to him we run for refuge'; 'بدویم' is an old-form 1st‑person plural verb meaning 'we run/hasten to' (compound of 'به + دو' in older usage) and 'پناه' is the object 'refuge.'
segments.s055-g35: The prepositional phrase 'به راه فریدون' = 'onto/in the way of Fereydun' (directional 'به' + 'راه'). 'فرخ' is a postpositive epithet 'fortunate' modifying 'فریدون' (or the way), and 'رویم' is a 1st‑person plural verbal form meaning 'we go'/'let us go' (classical/subjunctive/indicative form). Word order places the verb at the end, typical of Persian poetry.
segments.s055-g36: 'نیامان' here is the noun 'our ancestors' (نیا + plural ending) and 'کهن' is the adjective 'ancient/old.' 'بود‌گر' is an archaic fused conditional particle/verb meaning 'if it were' or serving a concessive/conditional sense; the clause finishes with 'ما نویم' = 'we are new' (نو + ـیم), so the line contrasts ancestral antiquity with the present novelty: literally, 'the ancestors were ancient, if we are new' → 'though we are new, the ancestors were old.'
segments.s055-g37: Fixed relative formula 'هر آن کس که' = 'whoever/anyone who'. The locative 'در هفت کشور زمین' places the action 'in the seven countries (realms) of the earth' — ordinal/quantifier + noun + domain. This segment sets the subject and location for the following verb clause.
segments.s055-g38: 'بگردد' is 3rd‑person singular present/subjunctive of گشتن used here in the sense 'wanders/roams/strays.' 'ز راه' 'from the way' marks departure from the right path; the conjunction 'و' links the second verb 'بتابد' (3rd‑person present of تابیدن) used here to mean 'turns' or 'is turned away' and takes the same construction 'ز دین' = 'from religion/faith.' The verbs are general/potential statements about anyone who does the roaming.
segments.s055-g39: 'نماینده رنج درویش را' is a noun phrase with 'را' marking it as a definite object/topic: 'the manifestation/representation (نماینده) of the dervish's suffering (رنج درویش).' The genitive relation between 'رنج' and 'درویش' is implicit (no ezāfe written in orthography here because of poetry), so this phrase names what is being manifested.
segments.s055-g40: 'زبون داشتن' is an idiomatic construction meaning 'to make abject / to keep humiliated.' Here 'مردم خویش را' is the object: 'one's own people' — 'خویش' = 'one's own' with the possessive suffix 'ش' (here segmented as 'خوی' + 'ش'). The verb 'داشتن' functions as the light verb 'to have/hold' in the compound and 'را' marks the definite object. Overall: 'to hold one's people abased.'

sentences.s055-s41: When Sam withdrew from before his throne, the champions set their steps after him (i.e. followed him).
sentences.s055-s42: He strode proudly and turned toward the tomb; the world acted according to custom and way.
sentences.s055-s33: You are my relic from antiquity; upon the Kayan throne you are my spring.
sentences.s055-s34: In battle you are an enduring lion; in the banquet you are a radiant beloved.
sentences.s055-s35: May earth and time be dust at your feet; may that very turquoise throne be your seat.
sentences.s055-s36: You have cleansed the land with the Indian sword; sit down in peace and choose tranquility.
sentences.s055-s21: Raising his head in pride and hoarding treasure, he became a source of suffering for his ailing people.
sentences.s055-s22: All, in my view, are downright unbelievers, and—because of Ahriman—are worse evildoers.
sentences.s055-s23: Whoever was not of this religion was accursed by God and cursed in his disposition.
sentences.s055-s24: And thereafter I will, by the sword, stretch out my hand and subdue the whole country and its low border.
sentences.s055-s25: All the heroes on earth cried "Bravo" to Manuchehr.
sentences.s055-s26: That (is), your fortunate ancestor — O benevolent one — gave you kingship, a throne, and a crown.
sentences.s055-s27: May you have an eternal throne (that repels foes) — the same crown and also the farr of the mobeds.
sentences.s055-s28: Each of our hearts is at your command; likewise our very lives are under your pledge.
sentences.s055-s9: I am the dark night that seeks vengeance; I am that keen fire of Barzin.
sentences.s055-s10: Lord of the sword and of the golden shoe, raiser of the Kayanian banner.
sentences.s055-s11: A bright blade and a cutting sword; I have no reluctance within my soul about going to battle.
sentences.s055-s12: Sometimes the sea's revelry is in my two hands; the breath of fire sits at my side.
sentences.s055-s1: Manuchehr was in pain for a week; his two eyes were full of water and his countenance was pale.
sentences.s055-s2: On the eighth (day) King Manuchehr came; he placed that Kayanian hat upon his head.
sentences.s055-s3: All the champions on earth cried out to him together, 'Bravo!'
sentences.s055-s4: When he assumed the (rustic) kingship upon his head, he announced good news throughout the whole world.
sentences.s055-s13: Know that I will cut short the evil hand; I will clothe the earth in the color of vengeance.
sentences.s055-s14: Wielder of the mace and representative of the crown, making the kingdom shine upon the ivory throne.
sentences.s055-s15: With these arts I am but one humble servant; I am a worshipper of the World‑Creator.
sentences.s055-s16: All of us strike our hands upon our weeping faces; all our tales we recite from God.
sentences.s055-s37: From now on the whole turn for battle is ours; to you belongs a place on the throne, and joy and feasting.
sentences.s055-s38: May I go about the world and take one of the enemies into fetters for a while.
sentences.s055-s39: Your ancestor gave me heroism; your love and counsel gave wisdom to my heart.
sentences.s055-s40: The king praised him greatly and lavished on him many princely jewels.
sentences.s055-s5: In justice, in rite/custom, and in manliness; in goodness, purity, and wisdom.
sentences.s055-s6: "I am," he said, "upon the revolving throne of the heavens; my purpose is wrath and war, and also justice and love."
sentences.s055-s7: The earth is my servant and the heavens my companion; the heads of crowned ones are my prey.
sentences.s055-s8: My purpose is the faith and my farr is godly; my lot is good fortune, and I am also wise.
sentences.s055-s29: The world‑champion Sam rose to his feet and said thus: "O Khosrow, justice is true."
sentences.s055-s30: Of the kings my eye is set upon seeing; from you is justice, and from us is approval.
sentences.s055-s31: You are the father of fathers, king of Iran; you are the choicest of horsemen and of lions (the foremost among riders and warriors).
sentences.s055-s32: May the pure God preserve you; may your heart be joyful and your fortune alert.
sentences.s055-s17: From him come crown and throne; from him our army; from him comes praise, and to him we hasten for refuge.
sentences.s055-s18: Let us go in Fereydun's fortunate way; our ancestors were ancient, though we are new.
sentences.s055-s19: Whoever wanders through the seven countries of the earth strays from the way and turns away from the faith.
sentences.s055-s20: To display the dervish's suffering is to keep one's own people abased.
